hints.genitive: Genitive would be 'iuventutis' — 'iuventus' with -us is nominative (4th decl. would be -ūs gen.)
hints.accusative: Accusative would be 'iuventutem' — 'iuventus' is nominative, the subject
hints.ablative: Ablative would be 'iuventute' — 'iuventus' is nominative here

hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'iuventutis' ends in -is, it is 3rd declension
hints.4th declension: Despite the -us ending, genitive 'iuventutis' (not -ūs) shows it is 3rd declension
hints.5th declension: 5th declension has genitive in -ei — 'iuventutis' ends in -is

hints.nominative: Nominative would be 'bellum' — 'belli' with -i is genitive singular
hints.dative: Dative would be 'bello' — 'belli' is genitive, dependent on 'patiens'
hints.ablative: Ablative would be 'bello' — 'belli' with -i is genitive

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'belli' with -i is 2nd declension
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'belli' with -i is 2nd declension
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -ūs — 'belli' with -i is 2nd declension

steps.reveal: patiens = enduring/able to endure (nom. sg., + genitive)

hints.perfect passive participle: Perfect passive participle would be 'passus' — 'patiens' with -ens is present participle
hints.3rd person singular verb: 'Patiens' ends in -ens, the participle suffix — a finite verb would be 'patitur'
hints.gerundive: Gerundive would be 'patiendus' — 'patiens' is present participle/adjective

hints.to open: That would be 'patere' — 'patiens' comes from 'pati' (to suffer/endure)
hints.to feed: That would be 'pascere' — 'patiens' comes from 'pati' (to suffer/endure)
hints.to feel: That would be 'sentire' — 'patiens' comes from 'pati' (to suffer/endure)

hints.pateo, patere, patui: These are the parts of 'patere' (to be open/exposed)
hints.pasco, pascere, pavi, pastum: These are the parts of 'pascere' (to feed/pasture)
hints.pando, pandere, pandi, passum: These are the parts of 'pandere' (to spread/extend)

hints.to go: That would be 'ire' — 'erat' comes from 'esse' (to be)
hints.to eat: That would be 'edere' — 'erat' comes from 'esse' (to be)
hints.to plow: That would be 'arare' — 'erat' comes from 'esse' (to be)

hints.present indicative: Present would be 'est' — 'erat' with the -ba-/-a- stem is imperfect
hints.perfect indicative: Perfect would be 'fuit' — 'erat' is imperfect indicative
hints.imperfect subjunctive: Imperfect subjunctive would be 'esset' — 'erat' with -at is indicative

hints.eo, ire, ii, itum: These are the parts of 'ire' (to go)
hints.edo, edere, edi, esum: These are the parts of 'edere' (to eat)
hints.aro, arare, aravi, aratum: These are the parts of 'arare' (to plow)

translation: Already first the youth, as soon as it was able to endure war
